intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond to irreverent humor and self-aware comedy in the voice rather than earnest motivation
facade.label: Dad jokes and puns
facade.acceptImplies: Embrace comedic, self-deprecating tone throughout the app—naming features after cooking disasters, making fun of recipe complexity
facade.rejectImplies: Stick to straightforward, helpful tone—focus on efficiency and clarity over entertainment value

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond to irreverent humor and self-aware comedy in the voice rather than earnest motivation
evidence.implication: Embrace comedic, self-deprecating tone throughout the app—naming features after cooking disasters, making fun of recipe complexity

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer single-number summaries over detailed nutritional breakdowns
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — whether users want one vital stat or comprehensive information
facade.label: Just the calories
facade.acceptImplies: Build around glanceable micro-info: calories, prep time, servings as isolated cards or badges. Hide complexity by default.
facade.rejectImplies: Users expect full context even if they hate cooking. Show macros, allergens, ingredients upfront. Data density is trusted, not feared.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer visual simplicity paired with food photography rather than illustrated or text-heavy layouts
facade.label: Minimalist cards with photos
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should use clean card-based layouts with real food photos to reduce cognitive load and increase appetite appeal
facade.rejectImplies: Either the card structure feels too sparse, or food photography isn't the right visual vehicle for this audience—perhaps illustrations or ingredient-forward visuals work better

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking might respond better to an interface that mirrors their chaotic reality—imperfect, cluttered, a bit lived-in—rather than sterile minimalism that feels like a professional kitchen.
facade.label: Messy counter
facade.acceptImplies: The app embraces human mess: scattered ingredients, rough textures, handwritten notes, visible imperfection. Signals warmth and permission to be sloppy.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want the app to be the OPPOSITE of their kitchen—clean, ordered, controlled. The app should feel like a refuge from chaos, not a mirror of it.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking need maximum focus on a single task — showing one recipe per view, full-bleed, with no sidebar distraction — to reduce cognitive load and decision fatigue.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense vs spacious / Scroll vs pages
facade.label: One recipe, full screen
facade.acceptImplies: User values immersive, linear flow over browsing multiple options at once. Single-task focus reduces friction for reluctant cooks.
facade.rejectImplies: User needs to see multiple recipes or options simultaneously, or wants quick recipe scanning. Layout should support comparison or overview mode.

facade.hypothesis: For a 'hate cooking' audience, progressive disclosure of ingredient lists (animate them appearing one by one) feels less overwhelming than showing all at once—reduces cognitive load and makes the app feel like a helpful guide, not a wall of text.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and behavior — animated reveal vs instant load
facade.label: Ingredients fade in
facade.acceptImplies: User finds staged, animated ingredient disclosure calming and game-like; app should lean into progressive disclosure patterns throughout (steps, equipment, timers).
facade.rejectImplies: User wants all information upfront, no animation; app should prioritize instant access, skimmable layouts, and no motion barriers to getting what they need fast.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want constant positive reinforcement and celebratory language, not comedic self-deprecation about failure
facade.axisTargeted: Tone spectrum: self-deprecating humor vs. motivational support
facade.label: Encouraging cheerleader
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into uplifting, congratulatory voice that treats simple cooking as genuine achievement worthy of celebration
facade.rejectImplies: Stick with irreverent, joking tone that assumes users find earnest motivation cringey or patronizing

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Recipes so simple, you'll actually cook them

Filter by mood

Today's picks

Garlic butter pasta

8 min4 ingredients

Boil pasta. Melt butter with garlic. Toss. Done. The kind of meal that feels like cheating.

Avocado toast

3 min3 ingredients

Toast bread. Smash avocado with salt. Spread. Eat standing up over the sink like a normal person.

Tuna salad sandwich

5 min5 ingredients

Mix canned tuna with mayo. Slap on bread. Add lettuce if you're feeling fancy. You're not.

Stuck?

Tell us what's in your fridge and we'll find something you can actually make.
